text: Authorize phase-two diligence only. No term sheet yet. Six issues remain unresolved: legal mapping, benchmark governance, counterparty and capital adequacy, real liquidity, competitive position, and management controls.

This is still a market-structure bet, not another AI app. If compute becomes financeable infrastructure, benchmark ownership and workflow penetration matter. Ornn is trying to pair a reference price with cash-settled products for providers, buyers, lenders, and traders.

There is enough public evidence to treat Ornn as more than a concept. Hydra brings a thirty-thousand-plus GPU data signal. Architect adds a pending exchange-traded futures path. Kalshi shows verification visibility. Bloomberg improves discoverability, but neither Bloomberg inclusion nor Ornn's reported four-hundred-plus users should be mistaken for independent validation.

Competition is already visible. Silicon Data has a live benchmark backed by DRW and Jump. Compute Exchange and Auctionomics add serious rivals. Public records still do not show the control bench expected for a principal-risk operator.

Legal contradiction is still the biggest gate. Marketing sounds like a regulated exchange story, while Ornn's own terms and swap disclosures narrow that claim sharply. Management still needs to produce a clean entity-by-entity legal map.
If Ornn takes principal exposure, underwrite it as market infrastructure, not software. Public materials still do not prove capital adequacy, segregation, hedge counterparties, or default management.

OCPI may be visible, but visibility is not underwritability. Public materials still do not prove governance, transaction-print depth, or market adoption, and competing benchmarks mean OCPI is not yet the accepted standard.

Proceed only with gated phase-two diligence. Advance only if management resolves legal mapping, proves benchmark governance, demonstrates liquidity, and shows capital and control discipline. Otherwise, pass.
